title: Shaun Edwards

}} }} '''Shaun Edwards''', [[Order of the British Empire|OBE]] (born 17 October 1966) is an English [[rugby union]] coach and former [[rugby league]] footballer, who is the defence coach for the [[France national rugby union team|France national team]]. A {{rlp|sh}} or {{rlp|so}}, Edwards is the most decorated player in rugby league history, with 37 winner's medals. In 2015, he was the 25th person inducted into the Rugby League Hall of Fame.{{Cite news|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/10064821/shaun-edwards-inducted-into-rugby-league-hall-of-fame|title=Shaun Edwards inducted into Rugby League Hall of Fame|work=Sky Sports|access-date=2017-06-05|language=en}} Edwards captained England in both rugby league and rugby union at schoolboy level. He played for [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[Rugby Football League Championship First Division|first division]] and [[Super League]] between 1983 and 1997, and also had spells with [[Balmain Tigers]], [[London Broncos#1994–2005: Broncos and Super League|London Broncos]] (twice) and [[Bradford Bulls]]. Playing for Wigan, Edwards won a record eight championships, and a record nine [[Challenge Cup]]s. In total he played in eleven Challenge Cup finals, also a record.{{cite book |last = Stanford |first = Peter |title = Why I am still a Catholic: essays in faith and perseverance |publisher = Continuum International Publishing Group |year = 2006 |page = 29 |url = https://books.google.com/books?id=GjPg3pPFtSAC |isbn = 9780826491459}} He was voted [[Man of Steel Award|Man of Steel]] in 1990 and is an inductee of the [[Wigan Hall of Fame]]. Edwards played 36 times for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]], as well as for [[England national rugby league team|England]] in 1995 and 1996 and [[Ireland national rugby league team|Ireland]] in 1998. In all, he appeared in three [[Rugby League World Cup]]s. After retiring from playing he joined [[Wasps RFC|London Wasps]] in rugby union as assistant coach in 2001, and was head coach from 2005 to 2011. During his time at the club, they won two Heineken Cups, four Premiership titles, an Anglo-Welsh trophy and the European Challenge Cup. In 2008, he became Wales' defence coach, and won four Six Nations championships, including three Grand Slams. He was also defence coach for the [[British & Irish Lions]] on their [[2009 British & Irish Lions tour to South Africa|tour of South Africa]] in 2009. He joined France in 2020 and won the Grand Slam in 2022 and the Six Nations title in 2025 and again, in 2026. ==Early life== Edwards was born in [[Wigan]], [[Lancashire]], England. His father, [[Jackie Edwards (rugby league)|Jackie Edwards]], played for [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]] from 1955 to 1964, as a {{rlp|so}} or {{rlp|sh}}, until a severe spinal injury ended his career prematurely at age 24.{{cite web|url=http://icwales.icnetwork.co.uk/news/wales-news/2008/06/13/wales-coach-tells-how-religion-gives-him-strength-91466-21067172|title=Wales coach tells how religion gives him strength|author=Lewis, Tim|publisher=Western Mail|date=13 June 2008|access-date=13 June 2008}} His uncle [[Bobby Edwards (rugby league)|Bobby Edwards]] played one match for Warrington in the [[Rugby league positions#Halves|halves]] against [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] at [[Wilderspool Stadium]] on Saturday 23 September 1961. Edwards was England schoolboy captain at both [[rugby league]] and [[rugby union]], and had been pursued by several clubs. His younger brother, Billy-Joe, also played rugby league for Wigan until his death, in a car crash, in 2003.{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/uk_news/england/2741899.stm|title=Rugby league players killed in crash|publisher=BBC|date=14 February 2003|access-date=20 February 2008}} His son James was an academy player at Wasps and played at scrum-half. Edwards is a [[Roman Catholic]], and his uncle, Fr John Johnson, was the sub-dean of Wigan and the parish priest of St John's and St Mary's churches in the town until he retired in 2023. ==Playing career== Edwards signed for Wigan in a blaze of media coverage on his seventeenth birthday; for a fee of £35,000,{{cite book |last = Houghton Mifflin Company |title = The Houghton Mifflin dictionary of biography |publisher = Houghton Mifflin Harcourt |year = 2003 |page = 482 |url = https://books.google.com/books?id=FRSe39hYzqMC |isbn = 9780618252107}} the largest in history for a schoolboy player. He made his début for the club at {{rlp|sh}} in their 30–13 home victory over [[York Wasps|York]] on 6 November 1983, 20 days after signing for Wigan. Later in the season Wigan reached the final of the [[1984 Challenge Cup]], and Edwards played at fullback in their loss to Widnes. In the [[1984–85 Rugby Football League season|1984–85 season]], Wigan reached the [[1985 Challenge Cup]] Final and Edwards played at {{rlp|fb}}, scoring a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in his side's victory. Edwards played in Wigan's [[1987 World Club Challenge]] victory over Sydney's [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]]. Edwards played for Sydney club the [[Balmain Tigers]] when they reached the [[1989 NSWRL season]]'s [[grand final]], for which he was selected on the interchange bench. In 1990, Edwards received the [[Man of Steel Award]] after he played most of the [[Challenge Cup]] Final against Warrington with a broken cheekbone and eye socket, after receiving a high, off the ball tackle in the 10th minute. He refused to be substituted and played on to set up three of Wigan's tries. Despite his performance, the Man Of The Match award went to his half back partner Andy Gregory. He played in Wigan's [[1991 World Club Challenge]] victory over Sydney's [[Penrith Panthers]]. Edwards finished the [[1991–92 Rugby Football League season|1991–92 season]] as the league's leading try scorer with a total of 40. He matched Wigan's record for most tries in a single match (10) in the 78–0 rout of Swinton in the [[RFL Lancashire Cup|Lancashire Cup]] 2nd round in September 1992.[http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=75 "RECORDS"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20101128221032/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/WContent.aspx?id=75 |date=28 November 2010}} at ''wiganwarriors.com'' It was a [[Rugby league county cups|County Cup]] record and record for a non-winger in any game. In addition, he scored four tries in a game on four occasions and hat-tricks seven times. During the [[1992–93 Rugby Football League season|1992–93 season]], Edwards played at scrum half for defending RFL champions Wigan in the [[1992 World Club Challenge]] against the visiting [[Brisbane Broncos]]. He played in Wigan's [[1994 World Club Challenge]] win over the [[Brisbane Broncos]] in Australia which attracted a World Club Challenge record attendance of 54,220 and also played in the first game of the 1996 [[Clash of the Codes (rugby)|cross-code challenge]] series against [[Bath Rugby|Bath]]. Edwards played in every round of Wigan's eight consecutive [[Challenge Cup]] wins. Altogether he made 452 appearances for Wigan, he played his last game for the club against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] in the Challenge Cup defeat at Knowsley Road in 1997. Edwards left Wigan that year to move near his son James, signing for the [[London Broncos#1994–2005: Broncos and Super League|London Broncos]]. He fell out with new coach [[Eric Hughes (rugby, born 1950)|Eric Hughes]], who refused Edwards permission to miss the first training session of each week, in order for him to spend time with his son in London. The decision to allow Edwards to leave the club led to multiple complaints from Wigan fans. After just a season in London, Edwards moved to [[Bradford Bulls]] but after only a few months returned to London, where he led the London Broncos to the 1999 Challenge Cup final at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley]].{{Cite news |last = news.bbc.co.uk |title = Cup heroes: Shaun Edwards |work = BBC Sport |place = UK |publisher = [[BBC]] |date = 27 February 2004 |url = https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/challenge_cup/3406135.stm |access-date = 6 March 2011}} He retired in 2000. ===International=== Edwards was the youngest ever player to play for Great Britain when he played against [[France national rugby league team|France]] in 1985. His political views meant that on a Great Britain Lions tour, Edwards taped over the [[British Coal]] logo on his jersey in support of the [[UK miners' strike (1984–1985)|miners' strike]]. He was selected to go on the [[1988 Great Britain Lions tour]], but injured his knee in the first game of the tour against [[Papua New Guinea national rugby league team|Papua New Guinea]], and took no further part.{{cite news |title=Edwards and Hampson drop out |work=The Guardian |date=3 June 1988 |location=London |page=23 |id={{ProQuest|186834655}}}} He captained Great Britain for the first time in 1990,{{cite news |last1=Fitzpatrick |first1=Paul |title=First cap for Cordle |work=The Guardian |date=6 April 1990 |location=London |page=20 |id={{ProQuest|187055027}}}} and was also selected to go on the [[1992 Great Britain Lions tour]]. He was sent off for a high tackle on [[Bradley Clyde]] in the first [[The Ashes (rugby league)|Ashes]] test match of 1994 at [[Wembley Stadium|Wembley]].{{cite web|last1=de la Rivière|first1=Richard|title=Shaun Edwards OBE|url=http://richarddelariviere.co.uk/?p=567|website=Richard de la Rivière|access-date=15 August 2015}} He played for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] 36 times, starting 32 games with a further four from the substitutes bench, and scored 16 tries. Edwards was England's captain for the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup|1995 World Cup]] tournament, but ruled himself out of the final against Australia with an infected knee.{{cite news |date=27 October 1995 |title=A fear of failure spurs Australia |url=http://newsstore.fairfax.com.au/apps/viewDocument.ac?page=1&docID=news951027_0181_4307 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140104212308/http://newsstore.fairfax.com.au/apps/viewDocument.ac?page=1&docID=news951027_0181_4307 |archive-date=4 January 2014 |access-date=4 January 2014 |newspaper=[[The Age]]}} ==Coaching career== In 2001, Edwards joined [[Wasps RFC|London Wasps]] in [[rugby union]] as a defence and backs coach, taking over as head coach in 2005 after [[Warren Gatland]] returned to New Zealand. Wasps won the [[Guinness Premiership|English Rugby Union Championship]] three times in succession, in 2003, 2004 and 2005, and the [[Heineken Cup]] in 2004. During his reign as Head Coach, [[Wasps RFC|London Wasps]] won the [[Anglo-Welsh Cup]] in 2006, the [[Heineken Cup]] in 2007 and the [[Guinness Premiership|English Rugby Union Championship]] in 2008. Edwards teamed up with Gatland again, after the latter was appointed head coach of {{nrut|Wales}}: Edwards had been offered the job of coaching England's second-tier side, [[England Saxons]],{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/main.jhtml?xml=/sport/2008/02/16/srmott116.xml|title=Fear of failure spurs Shaun Edwards|first=Sue|last=Mott|date=16 February 2008|newspaper=Telegraph|location=London}}{{dead link|date=July 2021|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}} but preferred the assistant coach position with Wales. Former {{nrut|England}} player [[Matt Dawson]] stated that it was "a crime" that England lost him to Wales and described him as "the best coach in the world".{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/welsh/7218043.stm|date=31 January 2008|title=Edwards is world's best - Dawson|publisher=BBC Sport}} Edwards left his position at London Wasps in November 2011.{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/nov/01/shaun-edwards-london-wasps-wales?newsfeed=true|location=London|work=The Guardian|first=Mike|last=Averis|title=England and Wales on alert as Shaun Edwards leaves London Wasps|date=1 November 2011}} Since joining the Wales coaching team, Edwards has helped the nation to Grand Slam wins in 2008, 2012 and 2019 as well the Six Nations title in 2013. Wales also reached the last four of the 2011 Rugby World Cup in New Zealand. In Edwards' first Six Nations Wales, he conceded just two tries on their way to the title and in 2013–14 Wales went more than 400 minutes in the tournament without conceding a try. He was named Rugby World International Coach of the Year in 2008.{{Citation needed|date=January 2019}} He was defence coach for the [[British & Irish Lions]] on their [[2009 British & Irish Lions tour to South Africa|tour of South Africa]] in 2009. On 8 August 2018, it was announced that Edwards would return to rugby league to coach his former club Wigan Warriors in 2020, after completing his commitments with Wales. He described it as an opportunity too special to turn down. However, in March 2019, he stated that he had not signed a contract with Wigan, despite already posing for press photographs and conducting interviews at Wigan's ground about this role, instead signing up as France defence coach early 2020.{{cite web | title=Shaun Edwards says he has not signed contract with Wigan | website=BBC Sport | date=2019-03-17 | url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/47604947 | access-date=2024-10-18}} His decision not to take the role at the Warriors was not popular with fans, and Edwards himself acknowledged it had affected his legacy at the club. ==Personal life== {{BLP unreferenced section|date=January 2019}} Edwards had a long-term relationship with [[M People]] singer [[Heather Small]], with whom he has a son, ex-[[Labour Party (UK)|Labour Party]] councillor and current [[London Assembly]] member [[James Small-Edwards]].{{cite news |last1=Topping |first1=Stephen |title=Heather Small and rugby legend Shaun Edwards' son elected as councillor |url=https://www.manchestereveningnews.co.uk/news/greater-manchester-news/heather-small-rugby-legend-shaun-23882125 |access-date=6 October 2022 |work=[[Manchester Evening News]] |date=6 May 2022}} Although no longer together, a key factor in his moving to the south was that he could be close to his son. When offered the job of coaching the [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain rugby league team]], he turned it down because it would mean being in the north a lot of the time, away from his family.{{Citation needed|date=December 2017}} He is the patron of Looseheadz, a charity raising awareness for mental health.{{Cite web|url=https://looseheadz.co.uk/foundation|title=Foundation|website=LooseHeadz - Tackle The Stigma|language=en-GB|access-date=2020-04-14}} ==Honours== ===Club=== '''Wigan''' *[[World Club Challenge]]
